title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
text:
22.5 Step 2: Correlation‑Based Integration
The simplest integration: which proteins correlate strongly with which metabolites?


Code
prot_m  <- assay(mae[["proteomics"]])
metab_m <- assay(mae[["metabolomics"]])

cross_cor <- cor(t(prot_m), t(metab_m), method = "spearman")
dim(cross_cor)


[1] 200 100


Code
top_pairs <- which(abs(cross_cor) > 0.8, arr.ind = TRUE) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(
    protein    = rownames(cross_cor)[row],
    metabolite = colnames(cross_cor)[col],
    correlation = cross_cor[cbind(row, col)]
  ) |>
  arrange(desc(abs(correlation)))

cat("Strong correlations (|r| > 0.8):", nrow(top_pairs), "\n")


Strong correlations (|r| > 0.8): 55 


Code
head(top_pairs, 10)


        row col protein metabolite correlation
PROT1     1   9   PROT1       MET9  -0.9370629
PROT59   59  93  PROT59      MET93   0.9370629
PROT148 148  68 PROT148      MET68   0.9300699
PROT110 110  30 PROT110      MET30   0.9230769
PROT38   38  59  PROT38      MET59   0.9160839
PROT108 108  37 PROT108      MET37   0.9020979
PROT19   19  69  PROT19      MET69   0.8881119
PROT126 126  52 PROT126      MET52   0.8811189
PROT165 165  92 PROT165      MET92   0.8741259
PROT86   86  13  PROT86      MET13   0.8671329



22.5.1 Visualise Top Correlations


Code
top_prot  <- names(sort(table(top_pairs$protein),    decreasing = TRUE))[1:20]
top_metab <- names(sort(table(top_pairs$metabolite), decreasing = TRUE))[1:20]

corrplot(
  cross_cor[top_prot, top_metab],
  method = "color", type = "full", order = "hclust",
  tl.cex = 0.7, tl.col = "black",
  title = "Protein–Metabolite Spearman Correlation",
  mar = c(0, 0, 2, 0)
)









Interpretation: Dark red/blue blocks indicate groups of co‑varying features that may share biological function.
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics

title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
text:
22.6 Step 3: Multi‑Block PLS‑DA with DIABLO
DIABLO (Data Integration Analysis for Biomarker discovery using Latent cOmponents) finds latent components that maximally separate sample groups while jointly modelling multiple omics blocks.

22.6.1 Prepare Data and Design Matrix


Code
X <- list(
  proteomics   = t(assay(mae[["proteomics"]])),
  metabolomics = t(assay(mae[["metabolomics"]]))
)
Y <- mae$condition

design <- matrix(0.3, nrow = 2, ncol = 2,
                 dimnames = list(names(X), names(X)))
diag(design) <- 0
design


             proteomics metabolomics
proteomics          0.0          0.3
metabolomics        0.3          0.0




22.6.2 Tune Number of Components and Features


Code
set.seed(42)
tune_res <- block.splsda(
  X, Y, ncomp = 3,
  design = design,
  test.keepX = list(
    proteomics   = c(10, 20, 30),
    metabolomics = c(5, 10, 15)
  ),
  validation = "Mfold", folds = 3, nrepeat = 10
)
tune_res$choice.keepX


For demonstration, we use fixed numbers:


Code
sgccda_res <- block.splsda(
  X, Y,
  ncomp  = 2,
  design = design,
  keepX  = list(proteomics   = c(20, 10),
                metabolomics = c(15, 8))
)

plotIndiv(sgccda_res, ind.names = FALSE, legend = TRUE,
          title = "DIABLO – Sample Projection")











22.6.3 Performance Assessment


Code
set.seed(42)
perf_res <- perf(sgccda_res, validation = "Mfold", folds = 3, nrepeat = 10)
plot(perf_res)




22.6.4 Circos Plot: Cross‑Omics Correlations


Code
circosPlot(
  sgccda_res, cutoff = 0.5, size.variables = 0.6,
  color.blocks = c("steelblue", "darkorange"),
  title = "Cross‑omics Feature Correlations (Component 1)"
)









Interpretation: Lines connect proteins (blue) and metabolites (orange) that co‑vary on the latent component. Thicker lines indicate stronger correlations.


22.6.5 Extract Selected Features


Code
selected_prot  <- selectVar(sgccda_res, comp = 1, block = "proteomics")$proteomics$name
selected_metab <- selectVar(sgccda_res, comp = 1, block = "metabolomics")$metabolomics$name

cat("Proteins selected on component 1:", length(selected_prot), "\n")


Proteins selected on component 1: 20 


Code
cat("Metabolites selected on component 1:", length(selected_metab), "\n")


Metabolites selected on component 1: 15 


Code
head(selected_prot)


[1] "PROT31"  "PROT17"  "PROT4"   "PROT169" "PROT32"  "PROT143"

title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
section: 22.7 Step 4: Pathway Enrichment for Selected Proteins
text:
22.7 Step 4: Pathway Enrichment for Selected Proteins
Map selected proteins to KEGG pathways to identify over‑represented biological processes.


Code
entrez_ids <- mapIds(
  org.Hs.eg.db,
  keys     = selected_prot,
  column   = "ENTREZID",
  keytype  = "SYMBOL",
  multiVals = "first"
)
entrez_ids <- na.omit(entrez_ids)

kegg_res <- enrichKEGG(
  gene           = as.character(entrez_ids),
  organism       = "hsa",
  pvalueCutoff   = 0.05
)

if (nrow(kegg_res) > 0) {
  dotplot(kegg_res, showCategory = 15,
          title = "KEGG Pathways Enriched in Selected Proteins")
} else {
  cat("No significant KEGG enrichment found.\n")
}



22.7.1 Visualise a Single Pathway


Code
top_pathway <- kegg_res$ID[1]
pathview(
  gene.data  = as.character(entrez_ids),
  pathway.id = top_pathway,
  species    = "hsa",
  out.suffix = "MS_integration"
)
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics

title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
section: 22.8 Step 5: Network Visualisation of Integrated Features
text:
22.8 Step 5: Network Visualisation of Integrated Features
Build a bipartite network of proteins and metabolites that are strongly correlated.


Code
edge_list <- which(abs(cross_cor) > 0.7, arr.ind = TRUE) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(
    from   = rownames(cross_cor)[row],
    to     = colnames(cross_cor)[col],
    weight = cross_cor[cbind(row, col)]
  ) |>
  select(from, to, weight)

g <- graph_from_data_frame(edge_list, directed = FALSE)

set.seed(42)
layout_coords <- layout_with_fr(g, weights = abs(E(g)$weight))

plot(
  g, layout = layout_coords,
  vertex.size      = 3,
  vertex.label.cex = 0.5,
  edge.width       = abs(E(g)$weight) * 3,
  main = "Protein–Metabolite Correlation Network (|r| > 0.7)"
)









Tip: Export for Cytoscape:


Code
write.graph(g, file = "integration_network.graphml", format = "graphml")
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics

title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
section: 22.9 Common Pitfalls in MS Omics Integration
text:
22.9 Common Pitfalls in MS Omics Integration








Pitfall
Consequence
Solution




Different sample orders
Wrong pairs correlated
Verify colnames(prot_m) == colnames(metab_m)


Omitting batch correction
Correlations driven by technical artefacts
Apply ComBat within each layer before integration


Overfitting DIABLO
Non‑reproducible feature sets
Cross‑validate keepX; keep components to 2–3


Ignoring missing values
Biased correlation estimates
Impute before integration (Chapter 18)


Mixed log/raw scales
Correlations dominated by high-abundance features
Log‑transform and scale both layers


Correlation as causation
False mechanistic claims
Correlation is hypothesis‑generating only

text:
22.10 Decision Flowchart for Integration Strategy


Code
flowchart TD
    A[Paired MS proteomics + metabolomics] --> B{Same samples?}
    B -->|No| F[Cannot directly integrate]
    B -->|Yes| C{Goal?}

    C -->|Explore pairwise relationships| D[Spearman correlation + network]
    C -->|Supervised classification| E[DIABLO block.splsda]
    C -->|Pathway mapping| G[KEGG enrichment on selected features]

    D --> H[Correlation heatmap / circos]
    E --> I[Sample plot + feature selection]
    G --> J[Dotplot / pathview]

    H --> K[Biological interpretation]
    I --> K
    J --> K

title: 22  Pathway and Network Analysis for Integrated MS‑Omics
text:
22.11 Exercises

22.11.1 Exercise 1: Build an MAE from Real MS Data
Using the msdata package, extract a metabolomics feature table (via xcms) and a proteomics table (e.g., from DEP). Combine them into a MultiAssayExperiment ensuring matching sample IDs.
# Your code here


22.11.2 Exercise 2: Compare Correlation Metrics
Re‑compute the cross‑omics correlation using Pearson instead of Spearman. How do the top 10 pairs change?
# Your code here


22.11.3 Exercise 3: Tune DIABLO by Cross‑Validation
Run block.splsda with validation = "Mfold" and nrepeat = 5 to select the optimal keepX for each block. Report the chosen numbers.
# Your code here


22.11.4 Exercise 4: Pathway Enrichment with Metabolites
Use clusterProfiler::enrichKEGG on the selected metabolites (by converting KEGG compound IDs). Are any pathways shared with the protein enrichment?
# Your code here

title: 23  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
23.1 Why Reproducibility Matters in MS Analysis
Reproducibility is a non-negotiable standard for computational mass spectrometry. A published analysis must be rerunnable by others — and by your future self — months or years later. This chapter covers the tools and practices that transform ad-hoc scripts into robust, auditable, and shareable pipelines.
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
23  Build Reproducible MS Reports

title: 23  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
MS data processing is highly parameter-sensitive: different peak-picking thresholds produce different feature lists
Bioconductor package updates can change results — dependency pinning is essential
Large datasets require pipeline frameworks to avoid manual reruns
Journals increasingly require code and data deposition alongside manuscripts
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
23  Build Reproducible MS Reports

title: 23  Build Reproducible MS Reports
section: 23.2 From Reproducible Projects to Reproducible Reports
text:
23.2 From Reproducible Projects to Reproducible Reports
Chapter 3 covered the mechanics of project structure, renv, targets, Docker, and continuous integration. This final chapter uses those tools from a reporting perspective: the report should read already-computed pipeline outputs, document the exact software environment, and point readers to deposited data and code.
A mature reporting workflow has three layers:








Layer
Owned by
Stored as




Computational environment
renv / container
renv.lock, Dockerfile


Analysis pipeline
targets
_targets.R, cached outputs


Human-readable report
Quarto
.qmd, rendered HTML/PDF



The report should not silently rerun expensive peak picking or feature detection. It should load validated outputs from the pipeline and make the final analytical decisions transparent.
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
23  Build Reproducible MS Reports

title: 23  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
23.3 Quarto for Literate Analysis Reports
Quarto (the system used for this book) is also ideal for individual analysis reports. Embedding code and narrative together ensures that the reported results are always in sync with the code that produced them.

23.3.1 Key report practices


Code
# In your report .qmd, load targets output rather than rerunning analysis
library(targets)
feature_matrix <- tar_read(feature_matrix)
annotations    <- tar_read(annotations)


---
title: "LC-MS Metabolomics Report"
author: "Your Name"
date: today
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
---

## Detected Features

```{r}
cat("Features detected:", nrow(feature_matrix), "\n")
cat("Samples:", ncol(feature_matrix), "\n")
```

## Annotated Metabolites (Level 1–2)

```{r}
annotations |>
  filter(msi_level <= 2) |>
  select(name, formula, mz, rt, score) |>
  knitr::kable(digits = 3)
```
crumbs: Part VI — Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting
23  Build Reproducible MS Reports

title: 23  Build Reproducible MS Reports
text:
23.4 Version Control with Git
Every analysis project should be a Git repository. Minimum practice:
# Initialise
git init
echo ".Rproj.user\n.Rhistory\n*.RData\ndata/raw/" >> .gitignore

# Always commit renv.lock
git add renv.lock _targets.R analysis_report.qmd
git commit -m "Initial reproducible workflow"

23.4.1 What to commit vs exclude



Commit
Exclude




renv.lock
renv/library/ (large, regenerable)


_targets.R
_targets/ store (large, regenerable)


.qmd files
Raw .mzML files (too large)


references.bib
Intermediate result RDS objects

23.5 Data Deposition and FAIR Principles
Published MS data should follow FAIR (Findable, Accessible, Interoperable, Reusable) principles.

23.5.1 Recommended repositories








Repository
Data type
URL

Proteomics raw data + identifications
proteomexchange.org

Metabolomics raw data + metadata
ebi.ac.uk/metabolights


MassIVE
MS data (any type)
massive.ucsd.edu


Zenodo
Code + small processed data
zenodo.org





23.5.2 Minimum metadata to deposit

Raw data files (mzML preferred)
Sample metadata table (ISA-Tab format for MetaboLights)
Analysis scripts or _targets.R
renv.lock
Processed feature tables

crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
text:
By the Numbers




23 numbered chapters (Chapter 1 through Chapter 23) organized into 6 thematic parts
Coverage spanning MS foundations, reproducible project setup, feature detection, identification, quantification, normalization, statistical modeling, and biological interpretation
Hands-on examples built around the core R for Mass Spectrometry ecosystem (Spectra, xcms, QFeatures, PSMatch, limma, mixOmics)
Dedicated chapters for spectral library search, biomarker modeling without data leakage, multi-omics integration, and reproducible reporting
crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
text:
24.2 What We’ve Covered

24.2.1 Chapter 1: What Is Mass Spectrometry?

MS Principles: What the instrument measures (m/z), ion sources, mass analyzers, and acquisition strategies (DDA, DIA, SRM/MRM)
Data Artifacts: Ion suppression, contamination, and the origins of missing data patterns



24.2.2 Part I: Foundations — Reproducible MS Analysis in R (Chapters 2–6)

R and Bioconductor for MS (Ch 2): Why R, core data structures (vectors, data frames, lists), essential Bioconductor containers (Spectra, MsExperiment, QFeatures, SummarizedExperiment), and a complete end-to-end pipeline walkthrough
Reproducible MS Project (Ch 4): Project directory layout, literate programming with Quarto, dependency locking with renv, Docker for full-stack reproducibility, pipeline automation with targets, and continuous integration
Import Data (Ch 5): The landscape of MS file formats (proprietary vs. open standards), mzML structure, PSI-MS controlled vocabulary, and loading data with Spectra
Data Objects (Ch 6): Core Bioconductor containers — Spectra, MsExperiment, SummarizedExperiment, and QFeatures — and when to use each
Initial QC (Ch 7): Annotating samples, visualising TIC and BPC, assessing retention-time stability, detecting blanks and batch drift, and generating a QC report



24.2.3 Part II: Feature Detection and Identification (Chapters 8–12)

Detect Features (Ch 8): CentWave peak detection, retention-time alignment with Obiwarp, feature correspondence with PeakDensityParam, peak filling, and extracting a quantitative feature matrix
Audit PSMs (Ch 9): Loading PSM tables, target–decoy FDR filtering with PSMatch, flagging failure modes, and building an identification report
Protein Evidence (Ch 10): The protein inference problem, peptide uniqueness, igraph-based protein groups, and razor-peptide aggregation to protein level
Annotate Metabolites (Ch 11): The MSI confidence level framework, adduct deconvolution with CAMERA, exact-mass database search, and isotope pattern scoring
Library Search (Ch 12): Cosine similarity-based spectral matching, building local libraries with CompoundDb, querying MassBank/MoNA, and interpreting annotation confidence



24.2.4 Part III: Quantification Workflows (Chapters 13–17)

Quant Proteomics Object (Ch 13): The QFeatures hierarchy and how to build it from PSM-level data
LFQ Proteomics (Ch 14): Filtering, normalization, peptide-to-protein summarization, IRS for multi-batch data, and PCA diagnostics
TMT Labeling (Ch 15): Reporter-ion quantification, purity correction, and TMT-specific normalization strategies
Targeted Quantification (Ch 16): SRM/MRM data structures, peak integration, and calibration curve analysis
Metabolomics Pipelines (Ch 17): End-to-end untargeted metabolomics with xcms, from raw files to a normalized feature table



24.2.5 Part IV: Matrix Normalization and Batch Correction (Chapters 18–19)

Normalize & Batch Correct (Ch 18): Log-transformation, median and quantile normalization, LOESS, TIC-based correction, and ComBat batch correction
Missing Data (Ch 19): Distinguishing MCAR, MAR, and MNAR missingness; imputation strategies (minimum, KNN, BPCA) and their downstream consequences



24.2.6 Part V: Statistical Modeling (Chapters 20–22)

Differential Abundance (Ch 20): Design matrices, limma empirical Bayes moderation, multiple testing correction, and volcano plots
Covariates & Repeated Measures (Ch 21): Mixed-effects models for longitudinal and blocked designs in MS data
Biomarker Modeling (Ch 22): Nested cross-validation, feature selection inside folds, ROC analysis with confidence intervals, and avoiding data leakage in high-dimensional MS datasets



24.2.7 Part VI: Biological Interpretation and Reproducible Reporting (Chapters 23–24)

Pathway & Network Analysis (Ch 23): MultiAssayExperiment for paired proteomics–metabolomics data, DIABLO multi-block PLS-DA, KEGG pathway enrichment, and correlation network visualization
Reproducible Reports (Ch 24): Parameterized Quarto reports, linking reports to targets pipelines, exporting publication-ready figures, and depositing data to PRIDE and MetaboLights

Package
Purpose

Core MS data infrastructure and spectral data handling
Spectra(), filterMsLevel(), pickPeaks()

Quantitative features for proteomics workflows
QFeatures(), aggregateFeatures(), filterNA()

findChromPeaks(), adjustRtime(), groupChromPeaks()

Peptide-spectrum matching and protein identification
PSM(), addFragments(), filterPSMs()

Core utilities for MS data processing
noise(), compareSpectra(), robustSummary()

Utilities specific to metabolomics analysis
mass2mz(), calculateMass(), adductNames()

Generic functions for proteomics packages
spectra(), peaks(), intensity()

Example MS datasets for learning and testing

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
section: 24.4 Best Practices for MS Data Analysis in R
text:
24.4 Best Practices for MS Data Analysis in R

24.4.1 1. Choose the Right Backend


Code
# Small datasets: In-memory for speed
small_data <- Spectra(files, backend = MsBackendDataFrame())

# Large datasets: On-disk for memory efficiency
large_data <- Spectra(files, backend = MsBackendMzR())

# Very large or processed: HDF5 for balanced performance
library(MsBackendHdf5Peaks)
archived_data <- setBackend(data, MsBackendHdf5Peaks())




24.4.2 2. Implement Quality Control

Check coefficient of variation (CV < 30% for technical replicates)
Assess missing value patterns
Monitor batch effects with PCA
Validate feature detection rates



24.4.3 3. Use Appropriate Normalization

Median normalization: General purpose, robust to outliers
TIC normalization: For consistent total signal across samples
Quantile normalization: When distributions should be identical
Internal standards: When available, most accurate



24.4.4 4. Proper Statistical Testing

Use limma for differential analysis (handles small sample sizes)
Apply multiple testing correction (FDR/Benjamini-Hochberg)
Check assumptions (normality, homoscedasticity)
Consider batch effects in design matrix
crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
text:
24.5 Reproducible Research Practices


Code
# Document your analysis pipeline
# 1. Record package versions
sessionInfo()

# 2. Use project-based workflows
library(here)
data_path <- here("data", "raw")

# 3. Version control your analysis
# Use Git for tracking changes

# 4. Create R Markdown/Quarto reports
# This entire book is an example!

# 5. Share data and code
# Deposit raw data in public repositories (PRIDE, MetaboLights)
# Share analysis code on GitHub
crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
section: 24.6 Future Directions in MS Data Analysis
text:
24.6 Future Directions in MS Data Analysis

24.6.1 Emerging Technologies

Ion Mobility MS: Additional separation dimension requiring new algorithms
Imaging MS: Spatial metabolomics and proteomics visualization
Top-Down Proteomics: Intact protein analysis without digestion
Data-Independent Acquisition (DIA): Comprehensive MS/MS coverage



24.6.2 Computational Advances

Deep Learning: Neural networks for spectrum prediction and identification
Cloud Computing: Scalable processing of large cohort studies
Real-Time Analysis: Online processing for quality control
Integration: Multi-omics data fusion (proteomics + metabolomics + genomics)



24.6.3 Community Development
The R for Mass Spectrometry initiative continues to evolve:

New backends for emerging data formats
Enhanced visualization capabilities
Improved integration with online databases
Better support for non-standard MS applications
crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
text:
24.7 Resources for Continued Learning

24.7.1 Official Documentation

R for Mass Spectrometry Book: https://rformassspectrometry.github.io/book/
Spectra Documentation: https://rformassspectrometry.github.io/Spectra/
xcms Documentation: https://bioconductor.org/packages/xcms/



24.7.2 Community

Bioconductor Support: https://support.bioconductor.org/
R for Mass Spectrometry GitHub: https://github.com/RforMassSpectrometry
Metabolomics Society: https://metabolomicssociety.org/



24.7.3 Publications
Key papers describing the R for Mass Spectrometry ecosystem provide deeper technical details and validation studies. Check package citations using citation("packagename").
crumbs: 24  Summary and Future Directions

title: 24  Summary and Future Directions
text:
24.8 Final Thoughts
Mass spectrometry data analysis is a rapidly evolving field. The R for Mass Spectrometry ecosystem provides a robust, flexible, and open-source foundation for tackling both routine and cutting-edge analytical challenges.
The skills you’ve developed through this book - from basic data import to advanced statistical analysis - will serve as a strong foundation for your research. Remember:

Start simple: Use built-in functions before implementing custom solutions
Validate thoroughly: Test your analysis pipeline with known standards
Document everything: Future you (and collaborators) will be grateful
Engage with the community: Share code, ask questions, contribute improvements

Thank you for joining this journey through R for Mass Spectrometry. Now, go forth and analyze!


Happy analyzing!
